turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in Case 14簫8913, Molina簫Martinez v. United States. Mr. Crooks.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: As this Court recognized in Peugh v. United States, the United States Sentencing Guidelines remain uniquely central to Federal sentencing even where the district court ultimately chooses to sentence outside the Guidelines. Because of the strong anchoring effect of the Guidelines, as also recognized in Peugh, the natural effect of an erroneously high Guideline range is to skew a defendant's sentence higher than it would have been under the correct range. Yet when the district court has elected to sentence within what it believes to be the correct range, it is typically very difficult to determine what the district court would have done had it been presented with the correct lower range.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Crooks, you didn't cite 18 U.S.C. 3742(f), and I wondered why, because it reads: "If the court of appeals determines that the sentence was imposed as a result of an incorrect application of the Sentencing Guidelines, the court shall remand the case for further sentencing proceedings with such instructions as the court considers appropriate." So this seems to say that, if the incorrect 簫簫 there was an incorrect application of the Guidelines, then a remand is mandatory.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: It 簫簫 it does appear to say that, Justice Ginsburg; however, I believe in this Court's decision in Williams v. United States, the Court said that Guideline errors are nonetheless subject to the normal doctrines of harmless and plain簫error review and not subject to automatic reversal.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And 簫簫 and so it's not clear that there was a Guidelines violation here. The Guidelines were incorrectly calculated, but it's not clear that at the end of the day there was a violation. I mean, is that really your response to Justice Ginsburg, or am I oversimplifying?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: There was a misapplication of the Guidelines, but harmless and plain簫error doctrines apply to determine the remedy for that violation or misapplication.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, there was a miscalculation. I'm not sure if there was a misapplication. That's 簫簫 I 簫簫 I suppose that's the issue in the case. Or 簫簫 or is this 簫簫

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: We believe 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: 簫簫 is this quibble wrong?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: We believe that there was a misapplication because the Guidelines were incorrectly calculated with respect to the defendant's criminal history. And that is the type of thing that the statute Justice Ginsburg was referring to says you should remand for, subject, of course, as this Court says in Williams, to the normal doctrines of harmless and plain error. And we're here today, of course, on a plain簫error case because we have a Guideline misapplication that unfortunately was not discovered by anyone below.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, you say that when there is a 簫簫 excuse me 簫簫 a miscalculation of the Guidelines range, that should give rise to a rebuttable presumption that the miscalculation affected the sentence that the judge imposed.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Alito.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: And what does that mean? Does that mean that the 簫簫 the burden of persuasion shifts to the prosecution?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: We believe that the Court's opinion in Olano actually supports a conceptualization that it simply gives the defendant an alternative way to satisfy his burden of persuasion, which is done in a generalized rather than a case簫specific way.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, suppose there's no evidence, as 簫簫 as will 簫簫 may very often be the case. Suppose the judge says 簫簫 imposes a sentence within what the judge believes to be the Guideline range but says nothing whatsoever beyond that, and it turns out that that is not the correct Guidelines range, so that there's no evidence one way or the other about what the judge would have done had the judge understood the correct Guidelines range. What outcome in that situation?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: In that situation, typically the result would be that the third prong of plain簫error review would be satisfied and the defendant would have shown an effect on his substantial right.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So that means that the burden of persuasion in that situation is on the prosecution.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Again, we may be quibbling about terms, but we believe that the defendant satisfies his burden of persuasion with generalized evidence tending to show the 簫簫 that the natural effect of a Guideline error is to affect the sentence.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I 簫簫 I don't think I understand the difference between general and specific. Evidence is evidence. And you draw inferences from all sorts of circumstances. So I don't know why you call this general. It's evidence. Okay? Let's assume the Guideline was 70 to 100. The erroneous Guideline was 80 to 100, and the right Guideline was 70 to 100. Would we 簫簫 you draw a general inference that the corrected Guideline would have made any difference on that sentence?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: We would, Your Honor. Our position is that anytime the range is not the correct range, there should be a presumption that it affected 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So what changed the judge's mind in terms of the facts? He gave you the max when it was 80 to 100. What's going to change his mind about 70 to 100?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Because the fact that the Sentencing Commission gave a different range in its expert advice and its expertise is something that this Court has recognized factors heavily into district courts' decisions.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Let's assume I don't believe there's a presumption, okay? It's very hard for me to understand what difference between 70 and 100 and 80 and 100 could make. In this case, I have a difficulty understanding what difference the 簫簫 the Criminal History Category would make. But I'll give you another example of overlapping Guidelines: a defendant who's never committed a crime and a defendant who has committed a crime. I would be more inclined to say that an inference from the facts is that a defendant who's never been committed of a crime, that the judge might take that into consideration, even in an overlapping Guideline case, and send it back. Because we don't know how much mercy that judge might have shown. But if a defendant like yours has many criminal convictions, I'd be pretty close to saying I don't think he's going to make much of a different choice.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: But on the other hand, Your Honor, in this case, despite Mr. Molina簫Martinez's criminal history, the district judge imposed the bottom of what he believed the Guidelines to be, despite the government's request for a sentence at the top end of the Guideline range. And our position is that the natural pull of the Guidelines is so strong, so influential, that it is going to pull the judge toward the erroneous range, and therefore 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Crooks, let 簫簫 let me ask you this: You fail to object to the erroneous use of the 簫簫 of the Guidelines. If you had objected, what would the situation be? You would have to establish that the error was not harmless, or the 簫簫 the government would have to establish that it was harmless, right? Would there be a presumption of nonharmlessness?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: I believe under Rule 52(a) there is in effect a presumption of nonharmlessness that the government must rebut. The government must show that the error was harmless.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: To show that the error was harmless is the same as saying there is a presumption that it wasn't harmless.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: I believe 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's 簫簫 that's not the 簫簫

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: 簫簫 the facts 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: 簫簫 not the way I normally talk. I would normally say the burden 簫簫 the burden of establishing it is on the government, but I wouldn't say there's a presumption.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: But 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The government is wrong.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: But if the government makes no effort to do anything with respect to harmlessness, then the case will be reversed, or there will be 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The 簫簫 the government 簫簫 the government says that you are, in effect, making this standard harmless. That you 簫簫 you admit that this is a plain簫error case, and yet, your presumption, effectively the burden is on the government, is making this into a harmless簫error situation.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: It is, but 簫簫 in a way, but only for the limited class of errors that are Guideline range errors. Under Rule 52(a), the government must show harmlessness for every type of error.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Under Justice Alito's questioning, he asked if 簫簫 isn't it true that you have the burden of persuasion at the outset to show that there was a miscalculation? Once you meet that, his question was does the government then 簫簫 I believe his question was 簫簫 does the government then have the burden of persuasion to show no error. He was careful not to use the word "presumption," or at least he did not use the word "presumption." Is that the proper way to think of this case in your view? Another way of asking the question is 簫簫 continues with what Justice Alito was inquiring: Do we need to use the word "presumption" here?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: I don't think the word "presumption" is absolutely necessary. It is the word that the Court used in Olano, but I think it 簫簫 it's really viewed more as who bears the risk of nonpersuasion. And what we're asking is that in the very limited context of a Guideline簫range error, the defendant should be deemed to have carried his burden by the generalized evidence that this Court recognized in Peugh v. United States, that the Guidelines do affect sentences and that Guideline ranges do affect what the district court does.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Where have we done that before? I mean, we had dictum in United States v. Olano, which said that there may be a special category. There may be a special category of forfeited errors that can be corrected, regardless of their effect on the outcome. But this issue need not be 簫簫 be addressed. And you're saying 簫簫 and we have not found any such category in the past. You're saying you 簫簫 we have finally 簫簫 finally discovered one category that meets that dictum in Olano.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Well, the lower courts 簫簫 this Court has not found a presumption since Olano, but the Court did give careful consideration to whether a presumption should apply to the particular type of error at issue in Olano. But more importantly, the lower courts have looked to what this Court said in Olano, and in a tiny handful of 簫簫 of errors, they have adopted just such a rebuttal.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's more important? That the lower courts have established the law for us?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: It's not 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It seems to me we establish the law, and we have never found anything to come within that language in Olano. And it's dictum, and maybe it's wrong.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: We believe that it's not wrong, and that the lower courts' decisions, of course, do not bind the Court, but they are expressive of the experience of the lower courts.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But, Mr. Crooks, if I could take you back to your answer to Justice Kennedy. I take it that you don't need to use the word "presumption," right? You're 簫簫 you're 簫簫 the burden that you have to carry in 簫簫 in a 簫簫 in plain簫error review is you have to show a reasonable probability. And your argument would work just fine if you dropped the word "presumption"; isn't that right? And you just said that the anchoring effect of the Guidelines creates a reasonable probability that the outcome would be different in a case in which the Court initially thought that the range was different from what it turned out to be. Sort of the end. Who needs the word "presumption"?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: We agree, Justice Kagan. Unfortunately, the Fifth Circuit did not, so. But we do believe that 簫簫 that however you conceptualize it, the draw, the pull of the Guidelines is because of the unique centrality of the Guidelines sufficient to establish the reasonable probability of a different result.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What good does it do to say that the Guidelines are advisory, which is what we have held, if, when you mistake them or when you don't apply them properly, you automatically get a reversal, which is what you're arguing here? Does that sound like an advisory Guideline?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Well, Justice Scalia, we disagree. It's an automatic reversal. Besides the third prong of plain簫error review, there are two other very stringent prongs, the second and the fourth that must be met before there can be a reversal on plain error. But with respect to the question of whether it makes the Guidelines any less advisory, we don't believe that it does. It simply recognizes the fact that even though they're now advisory, district court judges still find them influential and persuasive as this Court indicated they should do in Rita.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: How did this 簫簫 how did this error come to light? Everybody missed it in the district court. It started with the probation officer, the judge, and the defendant. How did 簫簫 how was it found out?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Well, actually, it was missed initially, even on appeal, because I wrote an Anders brief, and the defendant wrote a response and pointed out that one of my factual premises was incorrect.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So the defendant 簫簫 the defendant brought 簫簫 detected the error.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Correct. And when I saw it, I realized immediately I had made a mistake, and I filed a merits brief with the Fifth Circuit instead of an Anders brief. And it proceeded to oral argument in the Fifth Circuit. The government conceded that there was an error that was plain, but they disputed that it affected substantial rights because of the overlap of the Guidelines.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Your argument has some appeal on the facts of this case because the judge sentenced the defendant to a sentence at the bottom of what the judge understood to be the Guideline range. But would your argument apply to any sentence that was within both the original and the correct Guideline range? Suppose the judge here had sentenced your client to 87 months, which I 簫簫 I think is the top of the correct Guideline range. Would the same 簫簫 would you 簫簫 can you infer from that that the judge would not have imposed that sentence had the judge understood that 簫簫 the judge thought that the top was 96, judge chose 87, that happens to fall within the correct Guideline range. On those facts, would you infer that the judge would have imposed a lesser sentence?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Our answer is that whenever the range changes in the absence of any other indication, you should infer that the sentence 簫簫 there was at least a reasonable probability that the sentence would change. There may be other contextual factors besides the high end of the Guidelines that would allow the government to show that in fact it wouldn't have made a difference. But if I understand Your Honor's question correctly, with only the difference in the range, we believe that that is a situation where you should assume there's a reasonable probability.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Why would you 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I was 簫簫 I was just going to say under that 簫簫 I suppose the defendant really wouldn't insist on a resentencing if that were the 簫簫 those were the facts, would he? In other words, it was incorrectly a maximum of 87 and he got the maximum, you say, oh, well the correct one could give you up to 96. If I understand it, the defendant is not going to raise that objection, is he?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: If he believes that he could get more 簫簫 but if 簫簫 for example, in this case the incorrect range was 77 to 96. If he got 96, he might well insist upon appealing because he might think the judge would give me the high end of the correct range.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: How about 簫簫 I mean, the question is, if the judge 簫簫 the judge thinks it's 77 to 96 and chooses 87, which is within that range, and it turns out the correct range is 70 to 87, so the 87 is within the correct range as well 簫簫 it's the top of the correct range 簫簫 what 簫簫 what reason would there be to infer 簫簫 why would there be a natural probability that the judge would have given a lesser sentence if the judge thought that the top was 87 as opposed to 96? The judge obviously thought in that situation the correct sentence is 簫簫 is 87.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: With respect, I have to disagree with your premise because I don't believe judges just pick numbers. I believe they look at the Guideline range and typically determine whether the defendant should be low, middle, or high. So the Guideline range does make a difference. And it 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Here's an example: Suppose a person 簫簫 everybody 簫簫 presentence report, government, defense lawyer, prosecutor, everybody 簫簫 thought he's in column 2. Okay? He's not a first簫timer. And now it turns out they made a mistake. He is a first簫timer. And so being a recidivist, the presentence report says put him at the top of the range. Now it's a lower range, but they know he's not a recidivist. Depending on the circumstance they might say, Hey, no, put him in the middle of the first簫timers. I don't know. You don't know. They don't know. Nobody knows. And so the question is, we don't know, really, what the judge will do upon remand. Common sense suggests ask him. That's what it suggests. And so you're worried about whether you can work the language around to let you do that, what is common sense. I'll have some questions for the other side, which I suspect you can, but nonetheless, isn't that the issue?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: That 簫簫 that is the issue. And we 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Let's factor in the fact that you did not object. Surely 簫簫 surely there ought to be a penalty for that. We don't treat cases where there has been an objection the same as we treat cases where 簫簫 where there has not been. Sending it back makes total sense when there is an objection that's 簫簫 that's wrongfully denied.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But 簫簫 but where you or defense counsel has, in effect, assisted the Court's error, it seems to me we demand more.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: And the Court does demand more. It is much more difficult to secure relief on plain簫error review because of the other prongs of the rule.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But anyway, we've got progress, because it does make sense. And if we've got progress, it is not a punishment to the lawyer for not having raised it. It is a set of circumstances where the result will make sense without unnecessary administrative problems.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It's not a matter of punishing the lawyer, is it? It's a matter of establishing a system 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes, that's right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: 簫簫 that induces lawyers to make objections when objections are proper and 簫簫 and not to mousetrap the 簫簫 the Court into 簫簫 into error by 簫簫 by not 簫簫 by not objecting.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: But we have 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What are the other two elements that you think serve that purpose?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Well, first, the second and the fourth prong is the answer to your question. But first I have to say 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But what are the second and fourth prongs?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: The second prong is that the error must be plain, which means it's not subject to reasonable dispute.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, that's no big deal. It 簫簫 that doesn't satisfy my problem.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Well, many Guideline errors are not. We have had plain簫error relief denied repeatedly on the second prong. The fourth prong, that it must seriously affect the fairness, integrity, and public reputation of judicial proceedings, is another hurdle that the defendant must meet on plain簫error review. A person is not in a better position by not objecting. A person would be foolish not to make an objection that would lower the range because it's always better in a sentencing proceeding to start out with a lower range, even if you're going to ask for something below the Guidelines.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: It's not disputed here. Everybody missed it. You missed it, and you 簫簫 you were candid in saying even on appeal you were ready to file an Anders brief until the defendant himself.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. In fact I did file an Anders brief.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It's 簫簫 it's not enough that the lawyer did not intentionally do it. We 簫簫 we demand of lawyers that they do it. They're supposed to assist the Court. And where the objection has not been made, it's 簫簫 it's an entirely different case when it comes up here.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: But I would suggest, Justice Scalia, that it is still far more difficult to secure relief on plain簫error review, even with the tweak to the third prong that we're suggesting, than it is to just make the objection in the first place.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, can you give us an example of a Guidelines error that would qualify for 簫簫 qualify as a plain簫error except for its failure to satisfy the fourth prong of Olano?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Yes. In our reply brief on page 22, we cited the Fifth Circuit's decision in United States v. Duque簫Hernandez. And in that case, the Fifth Circuit actually pretermitted the first three prongs of plain簫error review and said even if you meet these, you lose on the fourth prong for three different reasons. First, the Court said that the defendant had in fact admitted the criminal conduct that underlay the controverted Sentencing Guideline enhancement. Second, there was an issue of quasi dirty hands because the defense attorney had failed to make an objection that the same defense attorney had made in a previous illegal reentry case. And third, the defendant had an extraordinarily serious criminal history involving drugs and guns. And that is an example, one example 簫簫 there are others 簫簫 where the Fifth Circuit has applied the fourth prong very stringently.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Who would apply 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, in that case would you still send it back to the district judge to ask him in the case you just put?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: The 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: We're trying to 簫簫 let's assume that we're going to give you some relief 簫簫 that was far from clear, but let's assume that. We're trying to find out what the rule ought to be, what the standard ought to be. In the case you just explained to us, in your view, should that case go back to the district court?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: The case that I just described?

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: I 簫簫 I don't know whether I necessarily agree with that application of the fourth prong of plain簫error 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Who applies the first prong in the first instance? Is it 簫簫

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: The court of appeals.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So you're saying that even if the court of appeals gets through the first three, it doesn't remand for the district court to 簫簫 to apply the fourth test. It 簫簫 the court of appeals does that itself?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Correct. And if the defendant cannot satisfy the fourth prong of plain簫error review, there is no reversal and no resentencing.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So I would think that the answer to Justice Kennedy's question is if the court of appeals really thinks it's not going to give relief because of the fourth prong, why would you bother remanding it?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Why 簫簫 why would the court 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Why would 簫簫 why would the court of appeals bother remanding it? They could legitimately say it doesn't matter. We're not going to remand it because whatever happens on remand, we're not going to give relief based on the fourth prong. And if that's the case, why would the court of appeals remand it at all?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: The court of 簫簫 if the defendant does not 簫簫 if the court of appeals is not satisfied that the defendant has met his burden on the fourth prong, it will not be remanded; the judgment will be affirmed. And I would like to save the remainder of my time for rebuttal.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And may I just ask you to answer the government's position that if there's no presumption, it's not in your question presented so 簫簫 so that you will lock into the way you phrased the 簫簫 the question, that "should an appellate court presume"?

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: Just very briefly, our answer is that the Court in the cases we cited in the reply brief has gone on to consider the merits of a case after deciding a legal question presented by the Petitioner. And certainly, the Court always has the power and the discretion to address a question, even that was not in the question presented or addressed in the court below. And here, the court below undisputably addressed it, and even if the Court does not endorse the presumption we ask for, it would be useful guidance to the lower courts on the application of the third prong.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Meisler.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This Court should not adopt the presumption that all misapplications of the Sentencing Guidelines are presumptively prejudicial, for three main reasons: First, a presumption is not supported by this Court's plain簫error precedence or general principles of appellate review for harmlessness. Second, presumption is unnecessary, because courts of appeal are capable of discerning prejudice on a case簫specific basis. And third, a presumption ill簫serves the policies 簫簫 policies that underlie Rule 52(b); in particular, they need to induce timely objections in the district court.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I 簫簫 I am concerned about getting hung up on the labels. Okay? If you 簫簫 so don't say it's a presumption. We still have, don't we, the question of what happens if nobody 簫簫 and who loses if there's no evidence to move one way or another in a tie? It doesn't matter if you call it a "presumption" or not. Who bears the burden of at least moving forward, and certainly the burden of proof, if one of these errors is on the record?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I think, Your Honor, this Court's decisions in Olano and United States v. Vaughn resolve that question, and makes it clear the burden is on the defendant under plain簫error review. That is what the Court has said is the main difference on prong three on the substantial簫rights prong between plain簫error and harmless簫error review.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But let's say that's right: Yes, the burden is on the defendant. But does the defendant meet that burden by saying, look, there was a mistake in the Guidelines; we know that there's an anchoring effect. That's what the Court said in Peugh. That anchoring effect creates a reasonable probability that the Court would have done something differently.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: And so our 簫簫 our answer, Your Honor, is that the 簫簫 there's no 簫簫 I guess what Mr. Crooks calls a "generalized showing." I think it has to be done on a fact簫specific basis.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That is fact簫specific. I mean, I have exactly the same question as Justice Kagan had. Forget all the jargon. You're a 簫簫 a lawyer. You want to prove that it rained at noon on the 5th of February. Your witness says, I heard pitter簫patter on the roof. I looked outside. Water was going by the window. Anything else? No. Well, it might have been the window washer, but there is no jury that couldn't find that it was raining. Same thing here. Case after case, this Court has said: We assume, we presume 簫簫 or they don't even use those words 簫簫 the Guidelines influence the sentence. That's what they meant by the word "anchor." If you look at the reality, whether they depart or don't 簫簫 whatever you call it now. There's some special word 簫簫 you can find evidence after 簫簫 of course, the Guidelines affect the sentence. Maybe there's an unusual case, and they don't. The reason the judge doesn't say anything is because in Rita we said that if you say nothing, Judge, then the appellate courts are to assume it was reasonable. And therefore, the judge now says nothing. He just applies the Guideline. So we'd have to overcome about five cases in terms of what we said if we are going to rebut the common sense notion that of course using the wrong Guideline had an effect on the sentence. If it's an unusual case, i.e. if it's the window washer, let the government show it was the window washer. But the normal case, pitter簫patter means rain, and the normal case is that the judge, when he uses the Guidelines, or even when he uses them as a basis and says I'm departing, that that made a difference.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Well, I think 簫簫 I think, Your Honor, there's a 簫簫 there's a number of points in there, and to my mind that's a question of how much weight to give the bare facts of the error. Of course the error in this case is you used the wrong range. And so I think you're 簫簫 I 簫簫 from what I understand Your Honor's position to be is that the bare fact of the error, the difference in the range, itself establishes an effect and substantial rights. That's not usually how we do it. We say you have to look at the particular facts and circumstances in the case. And I think Your Honor 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Can you 簫簫 can you give a concrete example of what you mean? I mean, in my 簫簫 my intuition is the same as Justice Breyer and Justice Kagan, that if you 簫簫 if you apply the wrong Guidelines, then there's a reasonable probability that he would have received a lower sentence. But you say he has to show 簫簫 what would he have to show, concretely? Give me an example of what is a reasonable probability that he would have received a lower sentence.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Right. Well, I think there's just two points to that, Justice Ginsburg. One is what the 簫簫 what the appellate court is going to have before them in every Guidelines簫error case, which is the nature and the magnitude of the error. The courts then know is this a career簫offender bump that changes the defendant's offense level and criminal history and could double his range? Or is it, as in this case, a one簫level movement on 簫簫 because of a criminal history scoring error that the Sentencing Guidelines, themselves, say you can offset with a departure because it leads to under representation of 簫簫 of the defendant's recidivist potential. So the court's going to know nature and magnitude. And by "magnitude" I mean, are we talking one level? Are we talking 16? Are we talking nine? So there are going to be many cases. I don't dispute that.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: There will be many. And there are many cases involving rain. Many. I mean, I don't know how many thousands, but there are many common facts in thousands of cases where the government itself uses very similar kinds of evidence in order to show that it was really raining. And here, what we're doing is we're using very similar kinds of evidence, namely, that he used the wrong Guideline in order to show that, in fact, better 簫簫 more probable than not, or pretty probable, or whatever reasonable probability, that it made a difference.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Say 簫簫 I mean, it's 簫簫 I 簫簫 I don't see this as special.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Well 簫簫 well, I think it is 簫簫 it was 簫簫 it is special, because, of course, it would be the first簫ever presumption of prejudice that this Court recognizes in 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, that's because you're using 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: The range 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: 簫簫 that's because you're using the term "presumption." You don't have to use the term "presumption." You can say that this defendant has met his burden of persuasion. We make the assumption here, we 簫簫 a few of us have practiced under the Guidelines 簫簫 maybe none of us 簫簫 that the Guidelines are the beginning point in almost every sentence. That's the beginning point, not the ending point, but the beginning point. And the question is: Once this is shown, like, at a minimum, can't you just ask the district judge? Now, if you want to say that there is sentencing miscalculations in 20 percent of the cases, that they happen all the time, that this is going to burden the courts, I suppose you can make that argument. But many circuits, or some circuits at least, have the rule; they just remand it, ask the district judge.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I'm not sure that's how plain簫error review has ever been conceived of, Justice Kennedy. I 簫簫 I mean, Your Honor asked before, what's our rule? You know, what is the rule the Court had announced in this case without calling it presumption? And if the Court wants to opine in this case about what the standard should be, we think the Court could say something such as that a difference in the range, the use of the wrong range, creates some likelihood in every case that the sentence will be different. But whether that likelihood arises to the required level of a reasonable probability is going to depend on the factors and circumstances.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, haven't we already answered that question in Peugh? In Peugh, the question was whether the likelihood was going to rise to the level of a significant risk. And we said yes. If you're using the wrong range, there is a significant risk that you would have made a different decision. So significant risk actually seems higher to me than reasonable probability. At least it's not demonstrably lower. So I would think that we've already said, if you're using the wrong range because of the anchoring effect of the range 簫簫 an anchoring effect that is kind of mandatory. It's 簫簫 you know, we say that these Guidelines are advisory, but we insist that a judge begin with them. So if you're using the wrong range, there is a significant risk. I would think so too, a reasonable 簫簫 of reasonable probability. Why 簫簫 why 簫簫 why is there a difference?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I 簫簫 this 簫簫 I think there's two points on Peugh, Your Honor. The first one is that Peugh itself, in footnote 8, says that this is a 簫簫 this is an ex post facto error that implicates constitutional concerns. And the Court said, in their regular Chapman v. California harmless簫error analysis, Petitioner concedes that when it's a nonconstitutional Guidelines error, under Williams v. The United States in Rule 52(a), you do the regular harmless簫error standard. We think it follows from that, that when the error has been forfeited, not preserved, and there's no constitutional concerns, do the regular plain簫error analysis. And I think it 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: In regard to 簫簫 are you telling us that even if there's a significant risk that this sentence was too high, in this case there can be no relief?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Not at all, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Is that the government's position?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Not at all, Your Honor. If 簫簫 if the defendant makes a case簫specific showing of a significant risk or a reasonable probability, then the defendant would clear prong three of the 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What the 簫簫 you think the two are equivalent? A risk and a probability are 簫簫 are interchangeable? We're 簫簫 we're 簫簫 we're going to change our 簫簫 our law now, so that plain error is overcome by just a significant risk?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I 簫簫 I don't 簫簫 I don't think so, Your Honor. And if I could just 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: No, but 簫簫 but 簫簫 but you've accepted it.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You seem to say that significant risk and reasonable probability are one and the same. I don't think they're one and the same at all.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: This was going to be the second part of my answer to Justice Kagan, which I actually think that the 簫簫 framing it in terms of risk, looking at how Peugh analyzed the issue actually lines up almost exactly with how this Court handled the error at issue in Marcus, Marcus in 2010. Marcus was about whether a defendant had been improperly convicted in violation of the due process clause based on pre簫enactment conduct. And this Court said the risk that's going to happen can be ameliorated by certain things that a judge does, but that risk is going to vary by case. And the Court said in that situation where there's a risk and it varies, we're going to hold 簫簫 require a regular 簫簫 the regular individual showing of prejudice. Now 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Now, does that individual showing, can it be made on the basis purely of the mistake in the Guidelines, there is no extraneous evidence? If you have a case where the erroneous range is 20 to 40 and the judge gives 20, and the correct range is zero to 20, is that a case in which the defendant has established plain error?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: With the caveat that we'd want to know a little bit more about the 簫簫 the facts and circumstances.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I don't want to know anything more about it because if the judge is within the Guidelines, he doesn't have to say anything more about it.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I think, Your Honor, if that's all you knew, if it was truly 簫簫 it's all you knew, I would say that the defendant probably has 簫簫 has met his burden in that case.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Okay. So now let's say 簫簫 I mean, this is why the problem is 簫簫 is 簫簫 whether you want to call it a presumption or whatever, is so difficult. What if the Guideline was 20 to 簫簫 to 40 and the judge said 30, and the correct Guideline was zero to 30? In other words, he's half in the middle of the wrong one. Do you say, well, maybe it's an error, maybe it's not? What 簫簫 what in that case?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I think I can give you a concrete answer without knowing more about the facts and circumstances. And that's not 簫簫 that's not 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: That's 簫簫 that's why I don't understand why it's one answer, zero to 20 and 20 to 40, and another answer when it's zero to 30 and 20 to 簫簫 when it's halfway as opposed to just at the bottom.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Right. Well, I think, Your Honor, the Court was faced with a similar situation in the Davila case from a couple terms ago, where the Respondent in that case had argued that 簫簫 and that was 簫簫 that involved judicial participation and plea discussions. And the respondent in that case argued, well, the courts of appeals are just finding all these prejudicial and reversing them anyway. Don't bother doing cases if they're prejudiced. Let's come up with an automatic reversal rule. And this Court said no. It said serious errors are going to be corrected on plain簫 and harmless簫error review, but do a full record assessment in each case. And that's really all we're asking the Court to 簫簫 to say here is full簫record assessment like in Davila 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But in 簫簫 in most Sentencing Guidelines cases, certainly in this case, the judge says nothing. He's told the probation office said these are the Guidelines, and the judge says, okay. I sentence him at the bottom, period. Doesn't explain why. And there's got to be many, many Guidelines cases where that's exactly what happens. The judge doesn't explain it. We have told the judge he doesn't need to explain it if he sentences within the Guidelines. So what do you do with what I think must be the bulk of the cases where the judge just sentences within the Guidelines and says nothing one way or another to explain it?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Well, I'm not sure, Your Honor, that's the bulk of the cases. If 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But you would agree that at least there are many cases where the judge just sentences within the Guidelines.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: There are many cases, but I think you run into the same problem as in Davila. You'd run into a problem of categorization. Are we going to 簫簫 are we going to change the entire framework, change the rules that we use in plain簫 and harmless簫error cases 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: We're not changing 簫簫 well, you've heard the argument. But I suspect 簫簫 and you 簫簫 you may know this empirical point better 簫簫 I think there probably are a lot of cases where the issue is the sentence. And the appeal will be 簫簫 include something like this. In some subset, there will be a plain簫error question. And all you'll really have is what the judge did. Now, of course, if the government has more, all you would be saying is that this, in and of itself, shows a risk in this circumstance, the reasonable probability, whatever standard you're using. Of course the government's free to show that there is something different about this. It wasn't harmful. It might have been the window washer, you know? And the government is free to show that.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: But 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So why is this so difficult?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: It's important, Justice Breyer, to recognize 簫簫 as I understand your 簫簫 your 簫簫 what you've posited, that is a shift in the risk, whether you call it the risk of nonpersuasion.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, you know, you 簫簫

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Absent the government coming forward with something else, the defendant wins. That's the exact opposite of what usually happens in the prong 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, you're saying it's a shift in the risk. The government has the burden of proving that it was raining. It's not a shift in the risk to say that the evidence that I've mentioned proves rain, and it's not a shift in the risk to say that the defendant can come in and show it was the window washer. The question is whether the burden, which lies with the defendant, is satisfied if all there is is that the judge applied the wrong Guideline. And given the continuous statement in case after case in this Court, that, of course, judges, whether they apply the Guideline or depart, the Guidelines play a significant part in the 簫簫 in the sentence that occurs. If all you have is that, you've satisfied the substantial 簫簫 whatever it is, you know. What is it called? The substantial 簫簫

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I think it does matter what it is, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Of course it matters what it is.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: It matters what it is. It's reasonable probability 簫簫 it's reasonable probability of a lower sentence.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, it doesn't say that in Olano. It says 簫簫 it says in Olano 簫簫

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: But 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 in Rule 52(b) 簫簫

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Yeah, substantial 簫簫 that the substantial 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It says "a plain error that affects substantial rights." And if it is the case, factually, as this Court has said it is, that applying the Guideline that you apply makes a difference to the sentence, that does affect substantial rights. He's just borne the burden of proof by showing that. That 簫簫 I don't see why that's different than any other problem of proof.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I think it's quite different, Your Honor. And if I could use the example of this Court's decision in Puckett v. United States, this Court has already spoken to how plain error works in one very serious sentencing error, the government's breach of a Guidelines簫related plea promise at sentencing in Puckett. And the Court said, not just, you do plain error; it works in the usual way. Because procedural errors at sentencing 簫簫 and the Court cited as an example, a misapplication of the career offender Guideline 簫簫 because procedural error at sentencing is amenable to harmlessness簫error review. And so we're going to hold the defendant to his usual burden because it's Rule 52(b), not 52(a).

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Don't you usually 簫簫 don't you usually have a plea colloquy or something in the record in those types of cases?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: You do. It's just that you have a sentencing 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, no. If it's 簫簫 the sentence is within the Guidelines, the judge doesn't have to say anything at all. So it's very difficult for the defendant to go back and say, here's what the error was even though there's also a clear error, a plain error in what the original calculation was.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I do want to dispute one other 簫簫 I think the key factual premise there. It's not that the judge said nothing at all. Depending on the spread of the range, by statute, the judge is required, this Court's decision in Rita requires that if the defendant makes a nonfrivolous request for a variance and the judge is choosing the Guidelines range, agreeing with the Commission's recommendation, instead of giving that non簫Guideline sentence, the judge has to explain that as well.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: If we 簫簫 if we accept the 簫簫 the proposition that in every case in which there is an incorrect identification of the Guidelines at the time of sentencing there has to be a remand, unless the government can prove with some sort of evidence that it was harmless, would there be any difference between the standard in a case where there was an objection and a case where there was no objection?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Not on prong three, Your Honor, as far as I can tell. The Petitioner's briefs have been a little bit less than clear on what exactly he thinks the government's burden is, but I heard him say today and I believe the citations at page 47 of his brief also indicate that the standard would be the Williams v. United States harmless簫error standard. The government would have to show no effect on the sentence. It has to persuade the court of appeals the sentence would have been the same absent the error.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But these 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but there are the other factors under the Olano test.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: There are, Your Honor. I 簫簫 I would agree that 簫簫 that prong two does some work in these cases, not as much in other settings because courts of appeals look at the plain language of the Guidelines and the application notes and believe that this follows from those 簫簫 that language. And more importantly on prong four, which Mr. Crooks mentioned earlier, he 簫簫 he would not embrace the Fifth Circuit's application of kind of a fact簫intensive, case簫specific prong four analysis. And the two courts of appeals that have adopted presumptions of prejudice on the Guidelines issue have both either 簫簫 well, the Tenth Circuit explicitly, it says, we presume at prong four as well. So I think it's very unclear whether prong four will do any work in these cases. One other thing I want to mention about these court 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: To the extent that our decision is influenced by our considerations of what kind of a burden, say the Petitioner's proposed rule would put on the resources of the Court, is there anything we can look to or can you or your co簫counsel or your colleague for the Petitioner tell us how often Sentencing Guidelines occur in 20 percent of the cases, 5 percent of the cases? And when they do, is it that disruptive to ask the district court, what would you do? Remand.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: A couple points. On the statistics, I believe they're collected in footnote 14 of Petitioner's brief, and there are something like 4,500 procedural sentencing appeals; about 2,400 of those are Sentencing Guidelines appeals, and about 20 percent of those, or about 480, are reversed. We haven't been able to ascertain how many of those are plain簫error cases versus harmless簫error cases, but it's a substantial number across the judicial districts every year. In terms of the cost, Petitioner's position is that it's less costly than remand for a retrial. We don't dispute that, but we do think it's not costless. The en banc Third Circuit, in a recent procedural sentencing error case, it reached 簫簫 explained it's not costless at all. You have to reconvene the parties, the judge, and a very busy 簫簫 especially in 簫簫 in Texas, a very busy district court calendar has to find a spot, reconvene the parties, transport a defendant from a distant location. And then, of course, we've collected in the appendix to our brief, situations where the remand for resentencing generates another round of appeals.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What you have not put in your brief, I don't think, is that they have, in several circuits, created a system where there is a limited remand for purposes of finding out just what the judge thought about it. That's not perfect, but it seems to be workable and not quite as expensive. But I think the real difference between us, in 簫簫 and you can 簫簫 I'm raising this because I haven't seen a reason you shouldn't address it squarely 簫簫 is I don't think this is a Rule 52(b) case. You see? It's not about how to interpret Rule 52(b), or 52(a), or Olano. It's a case about Guidelines. It is a case about what the Guidelines are and how they affect a sentence. And once we work that out, the answer will be obvious, because I'm not going to disagree with your interpretation of Olano or 52. But I am going to disagree about what you think the effect of failing to apply the right Guideline is in the mine run of cases. And once 簫簫 if you agreed with me about that, this case would be over; or if I agreed with you. Between us, we'd end it. But that 簫簫 that's 簫簫 so that's 簫簫 that 簫簫 that's where the 簫簫 I mean, the 簫簫 the others might have some views, too. (Laughter). But 簫簫 but do you see 簫簫 do 簫簫 do you see 簫簫 do you see why I'm 簫簫 do you see why I keep pushing you in that direction?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I 簫簫 I do, Your Honor. And I 簫簫 and I understand the point. I guess the 簫簫 the way I'd try to breech the gap between, perhaps, our positions is 簫簫 is to recognize and to argue Rule 52(b) is a capacious entire record, full簫record inquiry. And I think it leaves room; it accommodates judges' intuitions about how likely given errors are to have an effect in certain cases. And so our 簫簫 our basic position here, most fundamental position is don't blow up the Rule 52(b) framework. Don't blow up the Rule 52 framework this Court has applied in a number of cases and create, I think, two major problems. One is the anomaly I mentioned with Puckett, earlier. You're going to have courts saying, Well, the Supreme Court in Puckett said one kind of sentencing error doesn't get a presumption to the usual one, but it's a different kind of sentencing error in Molina簫Martinez, gets a different kind of treatment. That's a problem.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I see 簫簫 I see 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Does it matter 簫簫 does it matter that this kind of error is never strategic? This is not an objection that a lawyer would deliberately hold back. This is a 簫簫 a lawyer making a mistake by relying on what the probation officer said. I mean, there is practically heavy reliance on that presentence report.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: I think the issue, Justice Ginsburg, is really incentives at the margins, right, that in these kind of cases it's one 簫簫 and that's what both Dominguez Benitez and Puckett say. We want to incentivize lawyers to make these. And if, indeed, the prong three inquiry ends up being no different, the defendant's burden ends up being no different in this cost of cases under plain error than harmless error, it is marginally reducing the incentives 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You know, so much of this is the use of that word "assumptions," "presumptions"; but both sides are doing it. This case came up because the Third Circuit has a presumption that if a corrected 簫簫 it 簫簫 it says it: We assume that if Guidelines overlap, the wrong Guideline and the right Guideline overlap, that there is no way 簫簫 we assume there's no way you can prove error, or that it affected substantial rights. And there are a number of circuits who have said that. Overlapping Guidelines, you can never prove. That 簫簫 that seems that that's an assumption that's wrong, under your articulation of plain簫error review.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: If that's what the Fifth Circuit were doing, I 簫簫 I would agree with Your Honor. We don't think there should be a presumption either way. I think the Fifth Circuit has 簫簫 as other have 簫簫 circuits have 簫簫 have treated overlapping簫range cases a bit differently and have suggested that in those cases, because the judge's sentence still aligns with the Commission's recommendation, we're just going to require a bit more. I think what the Fifth Circuit has looked for is something more concrete that moves the dial from 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So let's go to Justice Alito's point. The more concrete here is that the parties were fighting about extremes. Sentence him, the government said, to the high end. The defendant was arguing he's entitled to the low end. There is some additional proof there. The judge picks the low end. If the Guideline is an anchor, which I think is almost undisputed, isn't that enough to say it affected substantial rights?

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: We don't think so in this case. I 簫簫 I would make two points: One is we're not abandoning our position that this fact簫specific issue is not properly before the Court. We would urge the Court to take a close look at Fry v. Pliler, which involved very similar circumstances. But on the merits, I think there are really 簫簫 both sides can point to kind of two factors in their favor on the 簫簫 on the plain簫error issue. The defendant can point to the factors that Your Honor mentioned. I think on our side is the overlap in the range and the nature of the error here. This is an error that can seem kind of abstruse. It's the 簫簫 it's because of the happenstance of when arrests and sentencing were sequenced. The defendant has two criminal 簫簫 two recent felony convictions that aren't counted at all, just drop out of the calculation. And the Commission realized that could happen in these cases, and so put in, not just a general departure note, but a 簫簫 a specific application note recommending a departure in this kind of a case when this kind of underrepresentation 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Except the judge 簫簫

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: 簫簫 happens.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 knew that he could have departed upwards under the old Guideline, because this criminal history was pretty serious. You argued he was entitled to the 簫簫 to the 簫簫 and should sentence at the upper end, and yet the judge sentenced him at the low end. So the 簫簫 the criminal history has less effect on the judge than it has on you, or perhaps on me.

turns.name: SCOTT A.C. MEISLER
turns.text: Right. Well, I think that maybe that's true, Your Honor. I 簫簫 I guess I would just say that I think these are all factors. And that if we're looking at case簫specific determination here, I think the court of appeals could reasonably conclude, kind of balancing those four facts I mentioned, that the defendant had shown a possibility, no doubt, of a low sentence. I think it's a close case, but not a reasonable probability. The last point I wanted to make, Your Honor, was just that we mentioned the courts' of appeals experience in this point. And I think it's useful to look at what happened in the Third Circuit in terms of whether this kind of presumption could be confined to the Sentencing Guidelines. In 2001, the Third Circuit, a decision called Adams, first announced it was going to presume prejudice for the denial of allocution, denial of a defendant's right to allocute at sentencing. A few months later, it adopted 簫簫 relied on Adams to find a presumption of prejudice for Sentencing Guidelines errors. The next year it applied those precedents to find a presumption of prejudice for constructive amendments to indictments. And in 2005, when Booker came down from this Court, it then applied those precedents to find a presumption of prejudice for Booker error for sentencing under mandatory Sentencing Guidelines. I think it's going to be very difficult to confine this. Mr. Crooks makes the point in his reply brief that the courts of appeals have been relatively restrained in finding errors. One can debate that, but I think the point is that this Court endorses the presumption of prejudice for the first time. I think it's highly unlikely it's going to stay just in the Guidelines context, just because of the anchoring effect that the Court recognized in Peugh. And if there are no questions, we'd ask that the judgment be affirmed.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Crooks, you have four minutes remaining.

turns.name: TIMOTHY CROOKS
turns.text: With respect to Justice Kennedy's question about the burden on the district courts of resentencings, we would point out that several lower courts in opinions we cited in the brief have pointed out that a resentencing is not all that burdensome, especially in light of the benefits of assuring that defendants are not serving more prison time than the district court wanted them to do. I did also want to address the government's point about 簫簫 the government touched on the issue of overlapping ranges versus ranges that don't overlap. And we just wanted to point out that the effect of the Guidelines is the same, regardless of whether the correct and incorrect ranges overlap or not. It's simply that the degree of the error, the amount of excess imprisonment that is produced, will be different. It will be lower in the case of an overlapping range. But as this Court said in Glover v. United States, any extra amount of imprisonment affects substantial rights. Finally, I just wanted to say that the courts of appeals have overwhelmingly recognized, whether you call it a presumption or not, that it is warranted to find that a change in the Guideline range affected a defendant's substantial rights because of the extreme likelihood that a Guideline簫range error will skew the sentence. And the lower courts have found, in their experience, that it's necessary to make that inference because of the great difficulty in the typical case of showing a case簫specific effect of the type that the government is arguing for here. And these two factors mean that this inference or presumption, whatever it's termed, is necessary to avoid, in many cases, the injustice of excess imprisonment. And it will not blow up Rule 52(b), as the government warns. It's a modest tweak to one prong of the rule that will help the rule serve its basic purpose of fairness. And for these reasons, we ask that the Court reverse the judgment below and remand for further proceeding.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
